Subject: Biology

Answer: Protein synthesis happens in the ribosomes, so it is where proteins are made.

Subject: Biology

Answer: (1) To strengthen and (2) support the cell

Subject: Biology

Subject: Biology

Subject: Biology
Question: What is a specimen? (1)
Answer: It is the object that you want to look at under a microscope

Subject: Biology
Question: What is a slide? (1)
Answer: A slide is a strip of clear glass or plastic which you put the specimen on

Subject: Biology
Question: What is a cover slip? (2)
Answer: (1) It is a square of thin, transparent plastic or glass (2) placed on a specimen to keep it from moving

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the purpose of iodine solution in microscopy? (1)
Answer: To stain the specimen so you can see the structures better

Subject: Biology
Question: Why must the cover slip be lowered carefully? (2)
Answer: (1) Because if you get air bubbles underneath, (2) it will obstruct your view

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the purpose of the cell membrane? (2)
Answer:
(1) To hold the cell together
(2) To control the movement of substances in and out of the cell

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the permanent vacuole? (2)
Answer:
(1) It is a space in the cytoplasm filled with cell sap.
(2) It keeps the cells rigid to support the plant.

Subject: Biology
Question:
If a cell is 20μm long and approximately 10 nuclei can fit lengthways. 
What is the approximate size of the nucleus? (2)
Answer:
20μm ÷ 10 = 2μm
So the nucleus is approximately 2μm in length.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does “order of magnitude” mean? (2)
Answer:
(1) It is how much bigger one thing is compared to another.
(2) It is shown in powers of 10.

Subject: Biology
Question: When writing numbers in standard form, what is the format? (2)
Answer:
(1) a × 10ⁿ 
(2) where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n must be a whole number

Subject: Biology
Question:
If a small animal cell is around 10μm and a large plant cell is around 100μm. 
How many orders of magnitudes bigger is the large plant cell than the small animal cell? (1)
Answer: The large plant cell is 10x bigger so it is one order of magnitude bigger, or 10¹ bigger.

Subject: Biology
Question: Why do prokaryotic cells have plasmids? (1)
Answer: For specific features like antibiotic resistance

Subject: Biology
Question: What is cell sap? (1)
Answer: A solution of sugars and salts

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the cytoplasm? (3)
Answer: (1) A liquid gel of nutrients and salts (2) which supports internal cell structures where (3) many chemical reactions happen.

Subject: Biology
Question: Name four structures found in all eukaryotic cells. (4)
Answer:
(1) Cell membrane
(2) Cytoplasm
(3) Nucleus
(4) Ribosomes

Subject: Biology
Question: How many orders of magnitude bigger is a small animal cell compared to a virus? (4)
Answer:
(1) A small animal cell is approximately 10μm.
(2) A virus is approximately 100nm = 0.1μm.
(3) So a small animal cell is 100x bigger than a virus
(4) which is two orders of magnitudes bigger or 10² bigger.

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the purpose of mitochondria? (3)
Answer: (1) It is the site of aerobic respiration (2) so it releases energy (3) for cell activities

Subject: Biology
Question: What are chloroplasts? (3)
Answer:
(1) Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll.
(2) Chlorophyll is green (it's what makes plants green)
(3) Chlorophyll absorbs sunlight so the plant can photosynthesise to make food.

Subject: Biology
Question: How could you use the size of cells to estimate the size of a subcellular structure? (4)
Answer:
(1) Compare how many times the structure fits into the cell
(2) Use the known size of the cell
(3) Divide the cell size by the number of structures
(4) This gives an approximate size of the structure

Subject: Biology
Question: What do all cells have? (4)
Answer:
(1) Cell membrane
(2) Cytoplasm
(3) DNA
(4) Ribosomes

Subject: Biology
Question: Describe how to draw a scientific diagram from a microscope observation? (7)
Answer:
(1) Drawing should be large (at least half the space)
(2) Use clear, continuous lines
(3) Do not shade or colour
(4) Keep structures in proportion
(5) Include a title
(6) Include magnification
(7) Label using straight, uncrossed lines

Subject: Biology
Question: Name five structures found in all prokaryotic cells. (5)
Answer:
(1) Cell wall
(2) Cell membrane
(3) Cytoplasm
(4) Circular DNA (DNA loop)
(5) Ribosomes

Subject: Biology
Question: Describe how to prepare a slide to observe onion cells? (6)
Answer:
(1) Add a drop of water to the middle of a clean slide
(2) Cut up an onion and separate it out into layers
(3) Use tweezers to peel off some epidermal tissue (thin skin) from the bottom of one of the layers
(4) Add a drop of iodine solution
(5) Place a cover slip on top
(6) (Stand the cover slip upright on the side, and lower it slowly to avoid getting any air bubbles underneath)

Subject: Biology
Question: Describe how to use a light microscope to observe a specimen? (6)
Answer:
(1) Clip the slide to the stage of the microscope.
(2) Select the lowest-powered objective lens (lowest magnification) 
(3) Use the coarse adjustment knob to move the stage up to just below the objective lens 
(4) Look down the eyepiece. Use the coarse adjustment knob to move the stage down until the image is in focus 
(5) Adjust the focus with the fine adjustment knob until the image is clear 
(6) If you need greater magnification, use a higher-powered objective lens and refocus

Subject: Biology
Question: What do most animal cells have? (5)
Answer:
(1) Nucleus
(2) Cytoplasm
(3) Cell membrane
(4) Mitochondria
(5) Ribosomes

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is blood?
Answer:
(1) It is a liquid composed of plasma (55%) 
(2) and red blood cells (45%)
(3) plus white blood cells
(4) platelets,
(5) and other dissolved substances transported around the body.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What colour is blood plasma?
Answer: (1) Yellow

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are platelets?
Answer: (1) Small fragments of cells (2) that start the clotting process when wounded

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe how platelets work.
Answer:
(1) When you wound yourself, enzymes convert fibrinogen into fibrin. 
(2) The fibrin forms a network of protein fibres. 
(3) These capture lots of red blood cells and platelets to form a jelly-like clot. 
(4) The clot stops you from bleeding. 
(5) The clot dries and hardens and becomes a scab. 
(6) This protects the new skin as it grows and stops bacteria from entering the body.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Where and how is urea formed?
Answer: (1) Urea is formed in the liver (2) from the breakdown of excess proteins.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Where is urea carried in the blood?
Answer: (1) It is carried to your kidneys (2) where it is removed from your blood to form urine.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How are red blood cells adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) They have no nucleus, (2) making more space for haemoglobin. (3) They are biconcave discs (4) which increase surface area to volume ratio. (5) They have haemoglobin (6) that binds to oxygen.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are the two types of white blood cells?
Answer: (1) Lymphocytes: (2) they form antibodies against microorganisms. (3) Phagocytes: (4) they engulf and digest invading bacteria and viruses.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Are white blood cells or red blood cells bigger?
Answer: (1) White blood cells are bigger

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Are there more white blood cells or red blood cells in the body?
Answer: (1) There are more red blood cells

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are the three main types of blood vessels?
Answer: (1) Arteries, (2) veins, (3) capillaries

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is the smallest blood vessel in your circulatory system?
Answer: (1) Capillaries

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How close are capillaries to your body cells?
Answer: (1) They are always within 0.05mm

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is the purpose of your arteries?
Answer: (1) To carry blood away from your heart (2) to the organs of your body.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How are arteries adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) Thick walls (2) containing muscle and elastic fibres (3) to withstand the pressure of the blood. (4) Strong and elastic walls to allow blood through them.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What colour is the blood in your arteries?
Answer: (1) Bright-red

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: When you feel your heartbeat, what are you feeling?
Answer: (1) The pressure of your blood (2) passing through your arteries

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is the purpose of your veins?
Answer: (1) To carry blood away from the organs (2) towards your heart

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How are veins adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) Valves (2) to prevent backflow of blood. (3) When blood flows backwards, the valves close.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How do valves work?
Answer: (1) When the blood flows backwards, (2) the muscles around the veins contract (3) and one set of valves close (4) to push the blood in the correct direction.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Do veins also have thick walls?
Answer: (1) No, (2) they have thin walls.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Do veins have a pulse?
Answer: (1) No, (2) there is not a significant amount of pressure.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What colour is the blood in your veins?
Answer: (1) Purple-red (2) because it is low in oxygen

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are capillaries?
Answer: (1) Tiny blood vessels (2) that form a huge network (3) to link the arteries and veins.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How are capillaries adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) One cell thick (2) for shorter diffusion distance. (3) Permeable walls (4) for diffusion.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is a lumen?
Answer: (1) The hole (2) in the middle of a blood vessel

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe the circulatory system of a human.
Answer: (1) It has a double circulatory system: (2) One carries blood from your heart to your organs and body. (3) One carries blood from your heart to your lungs and back.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Which mammals have double circulatory systems?
Answer: (1) Warm-blooded, (2) active animals.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is the difference between the pulmonary circulation and the systemic circulation?
Answer: (1) The pulmonary circulation involves the lungs. (2) The systemic circulation involves the rest of your body.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is the purpose of coronary arteries?
Answer: (1) To deliver blood (2) to the heart muscle

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How many chambers are in the heart?

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: When one side of the heart is filled with blood, is there blood on the other side at the same time?
Answer: (1) Yes, (2) the heart empties and fills simultaneously.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How does blood get from the atria to the ventricles?
Answer: (1) The atria contract (2) to force the blood down.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: When do the valves close in the heart?
Answer: (1) When blood is pumped out of the heart (2) via the pulmonary artery or aorta.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: When you hear your heartbeat through a stethoscope, what are you listening to?
Answer: (1) The valves closing

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Which side of the heart is thicker?
Answer: (1) The left side (2) because it needs to develop enough pressure (3) to force the blood through the body.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: If the right side of the heart were thick as well, would this be okay?
Answer: (1) No, (2) because the high pressure would damage the capillary network (3) where gas exchange takes place.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What happens to your coronary arteries when you have coronary heart disease?
Answer: (1) They become narrow (2) due to the buildup of fatty material (3) on the lining of the vessels

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Why is coronary heart disease a problem?
Answer:
(1) The reduced blood flow 
(2) means less oxygen is supplied to your heart 
(3) which can lead to pain, (4) a heart attack, (5) or even death.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are some methods of preventing coronary heart disease?
Answer:
(1) Stent: a metal mesh placed in the artery. 
(2) A tiny balloon opens up the stent and artery before being removed. The stent stays in place. 

(4) Statins: reduces blood cholesterol levels 
(5) and slows down the rate of fatty material deposition. 

(6) Bypass surgery: replace blocked coronary arteries with veins from other parts of the body.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Do you need a general anaesthetic to put in a stent?
Answer: (1) No

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Can stents be used with any other arteries in the body?
Answer: (1) Yes

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is a leaky valve?
Answer: (1) When heart valves leak (2) or don't fully open anymore (become stiff), (3) it makes the heart less efficient.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Why are leaky valves a problem?
Answer: (1) You can become breathless (2) and potentially die if not treated.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How do we fix leaky valves?
Answer:
(1) Replace with mechanical valves (2) made of titanium or polymers. 
(3) Replace with biological valves (4) from animals such as pigs, cattle or humans.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are the pros and cons of mechanical valves vs biological valves?
Answer:
(1) Mechanical: require medicine for life to prevent blood clots. 
(2) Mechanical: durable and last a long time. 
(3) Biological: work better and no blood thinners needed. 
(4) Biological: only last 12-15 years.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is your natural pacemaker?
Answer:
(1) A group of cells 
(2) in the right atrium 
(3) that control the resting heart rate.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Why is an irregular heart rhythm an issue?
Answer:
(1) If too slow, you're not getting enough oxygen. 
(2) If too fast, it cannot pump blood properly.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What is an artificial pacemaker?
Answer:
(1) Electrical devices (2) used to correct irregularities in the heart rhythm. 
(3) It is implanted into your chest.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: How do artificial pacemakers work?
Answer:
(1) They are attached to your heart by two wires. 
(2) They send strong, regular electrical signals (3) that stimulate it to beat.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: What are the cons of an artificial pacemaker?
Answer: (1) You will need regular check-ups

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: If your heart is failing, what can be done to fix this?
Answer:
(1) Wait for a donor heart that is a tissue match and get a heart transplant. 
(2) Get an artificial heart to keep you alive while waiting for a transplant 
(3) or give your heart time to rest and recover.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Pros and cons of heart transplant vs artificial heart?
Answer:
(1) Heart transplants not always available. 
(2) Artificial heart expensive. 
(3) Artificial heart risk of blood clotting.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe the structure of an artery in detail.
Answer:
(1) Thick walls
(2) Small lumen
(3) Thick layer of muscle
(4) Elastic fibres in the wall

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe the structure of a vein in detail.
Answer:
(1) Large lumen
(2) Relatively thin walls
(3) Often contain valves

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe the structure of a capillary in detail.
Answer:
(1) Very small / tiny blood vessel
(2) Narrow lumen
(3) Wall is one cell thick

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe how oxygenated blood passes through the heart (6)
Answer:
(1) Blood is oxygenated in the lungs
(2) Then enters the heart via the pulmonary veins
(3) Left atrium
(4) Left ventricle
(5) Leaves the heart via the aorta
(6) and is delivered to body tissues.

Subject: Biology
Subtopic: 2.2 Animal tissues, organs and organ systems
Question: Describe how deoxygenated blood passes through the heart (6)
Answer:
(1) Deoxygenated blood leaves body tissues
(2) Enters the heart via the vena cava
(3) Right atrium
(4) Right ventricle
(5) Leaves the heart via the pulmonary arteries
(6) Enters the lungs to be oxygenated.

Subject: Biology
Question: How are nerve cells adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) Dendrites (branched connections) for connecting to other nerve cells (2) Axons to carry the nerve impulse (3) Nerve endings/synapses to pass impulses (4) Lots of mitochondria for energy

Subject: Biology
Question: How far does the longest nerve cell run?
Answer: (1) From the bottom of the spine (2) to the big toe

Subject: Biology
Question: What are the two types of muscle cells?
Answer: (1) Striated (striped) muscle cells (2) and unstriated (smooth) muscle cells

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the difference between the two types of muscle cells?
Answer: (1) Striated (striped) muscle cells are used for movement (2) Unstriated (smooth) muscle cells are used for digestion

Subject: Biology
Question: How are muscle cells used for movement?
Answer: (1) Striated muscle cells form tissues called muscles (2) that work in pairs (3) to move bones of the skeleton

Subject: Biology
Question: How are muscle cells used for digestion?
Answer: (1) Unstriated (smooth) muscle cells control the movement of food through your gut (2) by contracting to squeeze the food along

Subject: Biology
Question: How are striated muscle cells adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) Lots of mitochondria (2) Special proteins that slide over each other (3) Store glycogen (4) Long

Subject: Biology
Question: What is glycogen?
Answer: (1) Stored version of glucose (2) that can be broken down and used for energy when needed

Subject: Biology
Question: How are root hair cells adapted for their function?
Answer: (1) Large surface area (2) Mitochondria (3) Large permanent vacuole to increase rate of osmosis

Subject: Biology
Question: How are photosynthetic cells adapted for their function?
Answer:
(1) Chloroplasts which contain chlorophyll 
(2) Arranged in layers in leaves and along outer part of stem to absorb more sunlight 

(3) Large permanent vacuole to keep leaves rigid 
(4) so they can spread out and capture as much light as possible

Subject: Biology
Question: How is a xylem cell adapted for its function?
Answer:
(1) The cells are dead and joined together 
(2) with no end walls between the cells 
(3) so they act as long hollow tubes to transport water and mineral ions 
(4) Lignin between the cell walls make the cell strong 
(5) to withstand water pressure and support the stem

Subject: Biology
Question: Are xylem cells dead?
Answer: (1) They are alive at first (2) then die when they mature

Subject: Biology
Question: How is phloem adapted for its function?
Answer: (1) The phloem cells don't have end walls (2) and have a special sieve plate instead (3) This turns them into tubes that can carry water with dissolved food (4) Phloem cells lose a lot of their internal structures to create space (5) They are kept alive by companion cells (6) which produce energy from their mitochondria and transfer it to the phloem

Subject: Biology
Question: How are plant roots adapted for efficient mineral exchange?
Answer:
(1) Root hair cells have a large surface area.
(2) Water is constantly moved away from the roots by the transpiration stream, maintaining a steep concentration gradient.

Subject: Biology
Question: Why do multicellular organisms require different systems?
Answer:
(1) Multicellular organisms cannot rely on simple diffusion alone. 
(2) Gases would take too long to reach every cell. 
(3) Food molecules would take too long to reach every cell. 
(4) Metabolic waste would take too long to leave cells (which could build up and poison them).

Subject: Biology
Question: How do fish maintain an efficient gas exchange system?
Answer:
(1) Gills are made up of many thin filaments. 
(2) The filaments have a rich blood supply. 
(3) Fish pump water over their gills using the operculum. 
(4) This maintains a constant flow of water, keeping a steep concentration gradient for gas exchange.

Subject: Biology
Question: How are plant leaves adapted for efficient gas exchange?
Answer:
(1) Leaves are thin, giving a short diffusion distance.
(2) Leaves are flat and have a large surface area.
(3) Stomata allow gases to enter and leave the leaf.
(4) Air spaces inside the leaf increase the surface area for gas exchange.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s a tissue? (2)
Answer:
(1) A group of cells 
(2) with similar structure and function.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s an organ? (2)
Answer:
(1) A collection of tissues 
(2) performing a specific function.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s an organ system? (2)
Answer:
(1) A collection of organs 
(2) which work together.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does differentiate mean? (1)
Answer: When cells become specialised to carry out particular jobs.

Subject: Biology
Question: State the levels of organisation in a multicellular organism.
Answer: Cells → tissues → organs → organ systems → organisms.

Subject: Biology
Question: Name three organ systems in the human body.
Answer: Any three of: digestive system, circulatory system, gas exchange/respiratory system, nervous system, endocrine system, reproductive system etc

Subject: Biology
Question: How many micrometres are in a millimetre?
Answer: (1) 1000 micrometres.

Subject: Biology
Question: What are the two types of electron microscopes?
Answer:
(1) Transmission electron microscope
(2) Scanning electron microscope

Subject: Biology
Question: Can you keep electron microscopes anywhere?
Answer:
(1) No. 
(2) They must be kept in special temperature, pressure and humidity-controlled rooms.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does resolution mean?
Answer: The ability to distinguish between two separate points.

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the difference between the two types of electron microscopes?
Answer:
(1) Transmission electron microscopes give 2D images. 
(2) They have very high magnification and resolution. 
(3) Scanning electron microscopes give 3D images. 
(4) SEMs have lower magnification than TEMs.

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the difference between light microscopes and electron microscopes?
Answer:
(1) Light microscopes can only magnify up to about ×2000. 
(2) Electron microscopes can magnify up to about ×2,000,000. 

(3) Light microscopes are cheaper. 
(4) Electron microscopes are more expensive. 

(5) Light microscopes do not allow you to see all subcellular structures. 
(6) Electron microscopes allow you to see more subcellular structures, such as ribosomes.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does dilute mean?
Answer: A solution with a low concentration of solute / high concentration of water.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does concentrated mean?
Answer: A solution with a high concentration of solute / a low concentration of water.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s a solute?
Answer: The substance being dissolved.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s a solvent?
Answer: The substance that dissolves the solute.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s a solution?
Answer: A mixture formed when a solute dissolves in a solvent.

Subject: Biology
Question: What’s osmosis?
Answer: The movement of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does isotonic mean? (1)
Answer: External solute concentration = internal solute concentration.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does hypertonic mean? (1)
Answer: External solute concentration is higher than internal solute concentration.

Subject: Biology
Question: What does hypotonic mean? (1)
Answer: External solute concentration is lower than internal solute concentration.

Subject: Biology
Question: What is the difference between isotonic, hypertonic and hypotonic solutions?
Answer:
(1) Isotonic: external solute concentration = internal solute concentrations
(2) Water moves in and out at the same rate, so no net movement. 

(3) Hypertonic: solute concentration is higher outside, so water moves out of the cell. 
(4) The cell shrivels. 

(5) Hypotonic: external solute concentration is lower, so water moves into the cell. 
(6) The cell swells and may burst.

Subject: Biology
Question: Why do athletes drink sports drinks instead of water after competing? (3)
Answer:
(1) Replaces water.
(2) Replaces salts/ions/electrolytes.
(3) Replaces glucose.

Subject: Biology
Question: What happens if there is an imbalance of ions and water in the body? (6)
Answer:
(1) Too little water in blood / ion concentration too high.
(2) Cells lose water by osmosis.
(3) Cells shrink.

(4) Too much water in blood / ion concentration too low.
(5) Cells absorb too much water by osmosis.
(6) Cells swell and may burst.

Subject: Biology
Question: How and why are isotonic sports drinks useful after exercise? (2)
Answer:
(1) Similar concentration of salts + glucose to the body.
(2) Replaces water + salts lost in sweat.

Subject: Biology
Question: How and why are hypertonic sports drinks useful during intense exercise? (3)
Answer:
(1) Higher concentration of salts + glucose than blood.
(2) Glucose diffuses from small intestine into blood.
(3) Diffusion = high to low concentration.
(4) More glucose for respiration.

Subject: Biology
Question: How and why are hypotonic sports drinks useful for rapid rehydration? (4)
Answer:
(1) Lower concentration of salts + glucose than blood.
(2) More water in small intestine than blood.
(3) Water moves from small intestine into blood by osmosis.
(4) Replaces water quickly.

Subject: Biology
Question: Why would you drink a hypotonic sports drink? (1)
Answer: For rapid hydration

Subject: Biology
Question: Why would you drink a hypertonic sports drink? (1)
Answer: For more glucose for respiration.

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What are the three states of matter? (3)
Answer:
(1) Solid
(2) Liquid
(3) Gas

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is diffusion? (1)
Answer: (1) The movement of particles from an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration.

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is dilution? (1)
Answer: (1) Adding more solvent (usually water) to a solution

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is a solution? (1)
Answer: (1) A mixture of solute and solvent

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is a solute? (1)
Answer: (1) The substance being dissolved

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is a solvent? (1)
Answer: (1) The substance that dissolves the solute

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is a saturated solution? (1)
Answer: (1) A solution containing the maximum amount of solute

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What are the units of solubility? (1)
Answer: (1) g per 100 g of solvent

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is solubility? (2)
Answer:
(1) how much of a substance will dissolve in a given volume of a liquid
(2) g per 100 g of solvent

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What does a solubility curve show? (1)
Answer: Solubility in g per 100 g of water plotted against temperature

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is the solubility of a solid affected by? (2)
Answer:
Temperature
The higher the temperature, the more soluble

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Explain diffusion in terms of particles. (3)
Answer:
(1) Particles move randomly
(2) Due to kinetic energy
(3) From an area of high concentration to an area of low concentration

Subject: Chemistry
Question: How does temperature affect diffusion? (3)
Answer:
(1) Higher temperature increases kinetic energy
(2) Particles move faster
(3) Diffusion rate increases

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Explain how a solid becomes a liquid. (4)
Answer:
(1) Temperature increases
(2) Particles gain kinetic energy
(3) Move faster / vibrate more
(4) Until they break away from their positions

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Explain how a liquid becomes a gas. (4)
Answer:
(1) Temperature increases
(2) Particles gain kinetic energy
(3) Move faster / vibrate more
(4) Until they break away from each other completely

Subject: Chemistry
Question: A student completes an experiment to show what happens when aqueous ammonia (NH3) and hydrochloric acid (HCl) combined. What happens?
Answer:
(1) The particles of ammonia are smaller and lighter than the particles of hydrogen chloride
(2) so they diffuse faster and travel further.
(3) A white ring of ammonium chloride will form closer to the hydrochloric acid.

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe an experiment that proves diffusion in liquids. (4)
Answer:
(1) Put potassium manganate (VII) crystals in a beaker of water.
(2) The molecules will diffuse from an area of high concentration (where the crystals are)
(3) to an area of low concentration (the rest of the water)
(4) The liquid will turn purple.

Note: any experiment will work.

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is the solubility of a gas affected by? (3)
Answer:
Temperature and pressure
The higher the temperature, the more soluble
As pressure increases, gases become more soluble

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe the properties of a solid? (6)
Answer:
(1) Strong forces of attraction hold the particles together.
(2) They have a definite shape and (3) a definite volume
(4) Lattice arrangement 
(5) Dense
(6) The particles vibrate in their position

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe the properties of a liquid? (5)
Answer:
(1) Weak forces of attraction hold the particles together.
(2) They have a indefinite shape and (3) a definite volume
(4) Randomly arranged but stick close together
(5) The particles are constantly moving randomly

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe the properties of a gas? (6)
Answer:
(1) Very weak forces of attraction
(2) They have a indefinite shape and (3) a indefinite volume
(4) Randomly arranged but will always fill a container
(5) The particles are constantly moving (6) randomly

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Name the different changes in states of matter. (5)
Answer:
(1) liquid → gas: evaporation
(2) liquid → solid: solidification
(3) solid → liquid: melting
(4) solid → gas: subliming
(5) gas → liquid: condensation

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Explain how a gas becomes a liquid. (5)
Answer:
(1) Temperature decreases
(2) Particles lose kinetic energy
(3) Move slower
(4) Come closer together
(5) Forces of attraction increase

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe how to investigate solubility at different temperatures. (6)
Answer:
(1) Weigh empty evaporating basin / record mass
(2) Heat water to just above 30°C
(3) Add solute you are testing
(4) Stir until no more dissolves / excess solid remains / solution is saturated
(5) Cool solution to exactly 30°C
(6) Pour clear solution into evaporating basin (avoid transferring undissolved crystals)
(7) Weigh basin + solution / record total mass
(8) Heat gently to evaporate water
(9) Cool and reweigh
(10) Repeat heating, cooling and weighing until mass is constant
(11) Use recorded masses to calculate solubility

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Explain how a liquid becomes a solid. (6)
Answer:
(1) Temperature decreases
(2) Particles lose kinetic energy
(3) Move slower
(4) Come closer together
(5) Forces of attraction increase
(6) Until strong bond formed

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe an experiment that proves diffusion using bromine gas. (6)
Answer:
(1) Bromine is brown and smells really strong.
(2) Half-fill a gas jar with bromine gas, and the other with air
(3) Separate the glass plate in the middle
(4) The bromine particles will move from an area of high concentration (where it is) to an area of low concentration (the side with air).
(5) And the air particles will from from an area of high concentration (where it is) to an area of low concentration (the side with bromine gas).
(6) You will get a lighter coloured gas which shows diffusion.

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is an atom? (1)
Answer: (1) Smallest particle of an element

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What subatomic particles are in an atom? (3)
Answer:
(1) Protons
(2) Neutrons
(3) Electrons

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What charge do atoms have? (1)
Answer: (1) Neutral

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is an ion?
Answer: (1) A charged particle formed by gaining or losing electrons

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is an element?
Answer: (1) A substance made of one type of atom

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is a molecule?
Answer: (1) Two or more atoms joined together

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is an isotope?
Answer:
(1) Atoms with same number of protons
(2) Different number of neutrons

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Describe the structure of an atom.
Answer:
(1) Nucleus contains protons and neutrons
(2) Electrons in shells around nucleus
(3) Nucleus is small and dense
(4) Most mass is in nucleus

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is the difference between atomic number and mass number?
Answer:
(1) Atomic number = number of protons
(2) Mass number = protons + neutrons
(3) Identifies element
(4) Determines isotope

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Why is relative atomic mass not a whole number?
Answer:
(1) It is an average
(2) Based on isotopes
(3) Different abundances

Subject: Chemistry
Question: Calculate relative atomic mass from isotope data.
Answer:
(1) Multiply mass × abundance
(2) Add totals
(3) Divide by total abundance
(4) Gives weighted mean
(5) Reflects isotope proportions
(6) Round appropriately

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is the relative charge of a proton, neutron and electron? (3)
Answer:
(1) Protons are +1
(2) Neutrons are 0
(3) Electrons are -1

Subject: Chemistry
Question: What is the relative mass of a proton, neutron and electron? (3)
Answer:
(1) Protons are 1
(2) Neutrons are 1
(3) Electrons are 0.0005
